sentences.text: That's right.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes.
It was wonderful, absolutely wonderful time.
And my cousins who were living in Birmingham-- they also enjoyed it very much because it-- it wasn't just an ordinary aunts and uncle because we all lived in Munich together, you see.
And that was wonderful for them as well.
We had Pesach together.
And furniture were very hard to come by, so people donated a bed here, a cupboard there.
And we found a-- a little flat.
And it was truly a wonderful time, first time to be together after five years and in freedom.
This was it, in freedom.
Freedom for you as well as your parents.
My parents.
Yes.
Yes.
We had a double freedom, and it was truly wonderful.
Yes.
After that, my parents integrated into the Birmingham Jewish, you know, community.
My father started to work again in the leather business.
He found a job to make leather purses and wallets and things.
And we lived.
And-- But something else that is peculiar-- I don't know if other people have mentioned it.
When we came as refugees, not only did not enough Jewish people take us into their homes, but there was also a slight-- I'm not talking about Polish and German background.
There was also a slight division between us, the refugees who had nothing, and the affluent, having become affluent English Jews.

Intermarriage, marrying into a-- a family who was already two or three generations-- I'm not talking about three generations, but even one generation because most of them hadn't come until after the First World War.
Very few had come at the turn of the century.
But they let us feel it.
We felt-- perhaps we might have been more sensitive than necessary.
We felt a bit of an under class.
So all the refugees stuck together.
We had our clubs, our groups.
And I was so happy to be part of the refugee scene after coming from those horrible people.
I was thrilled to bits.
I never noticed that we were sort of slightly looked down upon by the-- the English Jews.
They did a lot of good to us.
But-- there were some wonderful people among them.
And a lot of people-- you know, some of my friends were still sort of really refugees when my parents came.
They never had any parents.
And they had to make their own way.
But I don't seem to recall many marriages of-- of Jewish refugees to affluent English background people.
It really-- I mean, there's a most horrific story.
Always threaten my friend I will publish this.
I used to write.
I used to do quite a bit of writing for the And my friend now lives in the old age home in-- in London.
Excuse me.
She's a few years older than I.
To pinpoint what had happened-- I mean, that's a rarity.
I don't want to put it on record as being the norm.
No.
That is her story.
She was told-- she was a maid.
She-- a few years older than me.
She came on a domestic permit.
She was quite happy being a maid in a Jewish household.
Everything was fine.
And those Jewish people in Birmingham had their friends from London escaping the doodlebugs.
It was a lot more work for nothing.
Nothing was too much trouble for her.
Came Friday night-- I mean, she was part of the family.
There was whispering going on in the dining room.
She laid the table as usual.
Her boss came out and told her that his friends do not want to sit with the maid at the table.
Would she please eat in the kitchen?
She can come in to hear the blessings, to hear Kaddish.
But I've never got over that story.
I mean, I wouldn't say that was the norm.
Did you experience yourself anything like that?
No.
But we couldn't get any sort of English-- English Jewish boyfriends and things like that.
Excuse me.
I'm just going to-- [DRINKS]
So I-- I really noticed it a bit more later on.
My sister noticed it more.
She was younger.
She was when she came to Birmingham.
She went to school.
She was a top scholar.
She went to a-- She got another place in a grammar school.
And she never seemed to have made-- made friends with the English Jewish.
She said they were always a bit snobbish because we hadn't got their sort of-- the background.
Now, this is what I'm just trying to say.
Our background, our family history is at least as good as the people who grew up here.
But it wasn't acceptable because we were there the refugees.
Even our landlady-- and she was an old Miss, Miss [?
Melcher, ?] bless her-- memory.
She also talked about the refugees.
You know, they were a different breed.
Although she was very happy to give us part-- let us have part of the house as-- it was a self-contained flat.
But she certainly looked down on the refugees, not that she was anything very special.
She was a really poor old soul, but she felt that much higher, being English.
She-- Yes.
Yes.
We felt that much lower.
And-- but we were so happy.
We were so happy to have found our parents.
And my friends used to come and visit them.
And that's when I felt such a guilt complex.
The friend I was telling you about just now, [?
Irma, ?] and another friend who died about two years ago, a very close friend, [?
Louise ?] [?
Bergman-- ?] and in fact, [?
Louise ?] told me one thing that bears out the story I told you on the first tape.
She was a German Jewess, very Orthodox.
And she told me that in my first husband's house, she learned who were Polish Jews.
And they weren't perhaps too tidy, and my mother-in-law was hardworking and that.
She said that's where she learned about hospitality.
They had an open house.
They took in all the refugees for meals, for [INAUDIBLE], for Shabbos.

She said, never would I have believed that it would look-- in-- in-- all she heard in Germany was the dirty Polish Juden, the Polish Jews, from her parents and aunts and uncles who were very fine people, but they were-- she said, in Germany, I would have never been allowed to be a best friend to you.
She admitted it to me.
And she said true hospitality she learned from the Polish Jews in Birmingham.
So this is just to tie it up.
And my first husband came-- also from Polish background.
He came to England after the First War with his-- his brothers and his parents.
So they had truly an open house, and they were Polish Jews.
And there we never felt any-- being slighted or anything.
But a lot of the other Jews looked-- I think in a way, I think it must have faded by now because there's so much intermarriage now between them and our children and that.
So that doesn't count anymore.
But in those days, it did count, and we really felt it.
But I was too happy to feel anything like that.
I had my parents.
After what?
After Birmingham?
Well, I'm going to make a very big jump.
I-- I married in Birmingham.
I raised my--
I married Hanukkah time 1945, end of the year.
And that was wonderful.
And next year, I had my first child.
In the groups that I started to go to in Birmingham.
You know, there were some-- they were mixed already, some refugees, some English Jews background.
But it was the-- what was it called?
[HEBREW].
That's like the Orthodox-- a bit more Orthodox than [HEBREW].
And there were various groups in Germany.
There was the-- the lefties, like the Communistic group, more like the-- today.
Then they all went to Israel and started to build a kibbutz.
And because we were Orthodox, my parents wanted me to join the Orthodox group, which I did.
And I became great friends with my husband's family's cousins.
And another friend of mine told me also-- told me only recently-- that she lived in Birmingham.
I got to know her later.
She also felt that they were being looked down upon by the Jews of Birmingham who'd been in there for a generation or two longer.
So it's always-- everybody wants to have somebody to, you know, make [INAUDIBLE] over and look down on.
I suppose it is the natural course of events.
And I hope we'll never do that.
Oh, everybody-- we had a radio then.
We were posh.
We had a radio.
And we, you know, we heard the news.
And it was absolutely wonderful, the end of the war.
It was-- it was celebrated greatly.
I remember dancing in the streets.
And I remember I was working on war work.
As soon as I got to Birmingham, I looked out for the job.
Everybody had to do war work.
And I remember, I-- I think I stuck a pin in a-- in-- in a business you know, paper.
I don't think it was just at random.
It must have been a factory, a page with factories, my work.
And it was funny there.
I always-- although I say it, perhaps it isn't modest.
I'm a real hard worker, always have been.
And [LAUGHS] there the-- the non-Jewish girls-- they quite liked me.
But they said I'm greedy because I was-- it was all piecework.
And I was top, top, top.
I couldn't get enough work.
You know.
And I was asking if I can work the other machine.
So he said, no, it's not allowed.
They had a-- My milling machine was running so slowly, I was looking for another job, but there was a press, a hand press.
And I was working on that hand press.
So the manager came and said, that's not allowed.
I said, I don't want any money for that.
Just said, let me do it because I was so bored in that milling job run through.
Anyway, the other girls didn't like it.
They said, Jews are greedy.
They want, you know, too much.
They work too-- That was too much.
Before, they had a girl they told me about that she was bone idle, and she was Jewish.
So you couldn't please them, really.
But they were-- there I met a different kind of background English people, which I hadn't really encountered.
They were pretty.
They were wonderful.
They'd share their last slice of bread with you.
But they were very common.
And I was very innocently brought up, even by the people I stayed with-- I didn't learn anything of the world and the ways of the world.
And all of a sudden, I was thrown into that crowd of English factory girls.
And that was quite a revelation.
All sorts of things.
I learned-- [LAUGHS] Goodness, what I learned there.
[LAUGHS] Amazing things that girls worry about once every month.
They worried, and they said, why don't you worry?
I said, I've got nothing to worry about.
You know, things like that, which you came-- which were quite shocking to me.
Yes.
Yes.
Yeah.
They were showing all the lovely presents they got from the soldiers, from the American soldiers.
And, you know they-- they were laughing.
They thought I was very stupid.
I was 21 then.
Yes, I quite liked them.
We got on well.
So-- and then as soon as war-- oh yeah, my husband.
I met my husband before the war finished.
And he was-- he had a little food factory.
And because of that, he was exempt from war service.
And I wanted to help him instead of working in the war work factory.
And I couldn't get away.
And the day after peace broke out, I went to the boss.
And I said-- I gave him my notice.
He said, what do you mean, giving your notice?
I said, the war's finished.
I'm not working war work anymore.
He said, yes, but we've got to fulfill our contract.
And this is the very first time it's ever been brought home to me that people make munitions during peacetime.
He said, we haven't fulfilled our contract.
I said, I don't care.
I said, I'm not coming back anymore.
He sent the police after me.
But he couldn't do anything because in the meantime, I'd come out in a rash, went to the doctor, and she put it out to the factory and finished.
She gave me a medical certificate.
I didn't go back to work anymore.
But it was a shock to realize that you make munitions in peacetime.
Then I went to help my husband, who was by then my fiance.
I helped him in the business.
And that's-- that's how it was.
That was the most terrible thing.
I always say that in my talks that the worst time for the refugees was not the wartime.
A lot of people had-- had messages during the war from the Red Cross.
Everybody has hope.
But as soon as the war finished, the horrible pictures began to appear in the picture houses, in the newspapers, the-- the Jewish press.
It was absolutely horrific.
And that was the hardest time for the refugees.
It was dreadful.
And that's when the guilt complex started for people like me because we had been spared.
And most of my family, thank god, you know, was either in America from my mother's side, in-- in Israel then Palestine from my father's side.
And my old grandmother left Poland in-- just a few years before the war.
She went direct from Poland to Palestine.
And the only branch of the family that got left behind was one of her daughters.
I've never met her-- Sarah, Tante Sarah and her husband and children.
And the old lady never ceased crying for them.
She lived to over 100 in Jerusalem.
I never met her.
My sister did because she went to Israel when she was a youngster.
So that was the hardest time-- was peace for the refugees.
It took many, many years.
It's never-- people never readjusted.
We had breakdowns.
That I only found out 50 years later, you see, with the work that I'm doing now.
I got married.
I had a son, a wonderful son.
Danny was born 10 months after I was married.
He died sadly at the age of 21.
He became ill at the age of 20.
Never heard of such illnesses before.
In those days, you didn't even mention the word cancer.
It wasn't-- you know, you just didn't say it.
And then I had my second daughter, Shula.
She was born on the 18th of April, '48.
And Miriam, my youngest one-- she was born 30th of March, '52.
So that's the-- my family complete.
And they've provided me with-- I've got 10 grandchildren and three great-grandchildren.
So I've still got a lot, a lot be grateful for, even in spite of having had the tragedy of losing Danny.
After that-- I'm making a very big jump-- I came to London.
In 1979, I came to London.
My sister, really, and my parents lived in London.
And my marriage had finished, ended.
I think it was Danny's death that, you know, was the beginning of the end of our marriage.
He could never be reconciled.
To he-- you know, he became crazy.
He was religious to start with.
But he became crazy.
He said, it's the wife's fault if her son dies, and things like-- I mean, absolutely.
It was impossible.
He became really ill.
And after a while, I just had to leave.
And I had to put a long distance between us.
I lived in Birmingham for a couple of years.
But it was impossible.
You know, I wouldn't be living-- living in peace.
He was ill.
He was a good person.
But he became very, very ill within the nerves.
And he died in 1982.
That was two years after I was remarried.
So I live in-- I lived in London.
My sister found me a little flat.
I was quite a businesswoman by then.
I had taken over Danny's business.
Danny had started-- my brother was a manufacturing jeweler in London.
And Dan-- Danny became a traveler for him.
Or used to buy-- He became self-sufficient.
He used to buy from my brother and used to sell to the shops.
They did quite well.
And when he died, my-- my husband came completely unhinged.
And I took over Danny's business.
It was very difficult, very hard to go into the customers that he'd made.
And I did quite well.
In fact, I did it pretty well.
And I supported myself and my daughters and helped to make their weddings.
And my younger daughter lives in Israel.
She got married there.
And that's how I picked up the pieces again of life.
Apart from-- when I married again, I gave up my business.
My husband, my second husband, didn't like it.
He thought it was a bit dangerous, which it was by then, to be in jewelry.
And I sort of petered-- it petered out because my customers and I both became older.
And I didn't want to go and have new customers.
I used to buy and sell.
And I was bored.
I was absolutely bored stiff.
I'm an active person.
So what do I do?
I do this.
I do that.
I learned to play the piano.
Why?
Because [LAUGHS] my-- my daughter's-- my son-in-law's father once said to me-- in Birmingham, he said, what can't she do?
So I said, I can't play the piano.
So [LAUGHS] years later, when I had such a lot of time on my hands, nothing to do, I said, I'm determined.
I've got to learn.
I never-- I never learned to play properly.
Just a little bit, I learned to play the piano.
My husband was out in business all day.
And I did this.
I worked for the Ladies Guild.
And I worked for voluntary work and this.
Nothing fulfilled me until one day, 49 years after I'd arrived in Great Britain in England.
My-- most-- most everybody asked me, how did you start this reunion business?
My eye went around the room full of boredom because we were watching a television program-- very boring-- and it fell on the picture of my grandchildren.
In fact, that's the one.
She was then exactly the same age as I was when I left.
And I thought to myself, you know, you and your sisters and your brother and your cousins in Israel-- you don't know what your grandmother went through when she was just nearly 16.
You know, they're all spoiled, bless them.
They have this and that.
They have what they want and what they need and sometimes things they don't need.
And that's how it should be.
That's what we want for our children.
And then I suddenly realized, it's 49 years I've been in this country. 49 years, and nobody's doing anything.
Next year, it's going to be 50 years, a jubilee.
And I thought, nothing's been said in Nobody remembers the children who came.
And like a flash of lightning-- I'm very impulsive.
Terrible.
I can buy a car in five minutes and a flat in 10.
Honestly.
I see it.
I like it.
This is it.
I don't go hunting around.
I jump up off the chair-- I don't think my husband even noticed.
I phoned up my friend [?
Ilsa, ?] the one I told you about.
I said, [?
Ilsa, ?] do you realize we've been here 49 years?
Next year it's going to be 50 years.
Nobody's doing anything.
Nothing's being said or done.
She said, you know, you're right.
I said, I'm going to make a reunion.
I knew nothing.
I knew how many children came from Munich.
I knew us.
I knew-- and she lived in a hostel-- now, when the children came, half of them went to families.
Didn't mention the other half.
They could never place all the families with Jewish and non-Jewish people.
The other half went to hostels.
Most of them were run on very good lines.
And I always felt they were the happy ones because they had each other.
And they did better when the war ended.
They were still in hostels.
They survived the trauma together.
It was the people with the families who didn't do so well.
So anyway, I discussed it with her on the phone.
I said, I'm going to make a reunion.
She said, what are you going to do?
I said, oh, I live a few doors away from the synagogue.
That was in Stanmore then.
I said, I'm going to hire the synagogue hall, and we'll make a reunion.
Will you help me?
She said, yeah, sure.
She said, I'm going to America in a few weeks' time with my husband.
She said, I'm going to put a notice in the Jewish [INAUDIBLE], the German-speaking Jewish paper there.
I said, that's a bit silly.
I said, who's going to come from America?
Who is in America?
I thought we were all in England.
I thought literally there'd come perhaps 1,000 children, didn't know anything.
Anyway, she did it.
She went, and she did it.
And she said-- so she belongs to the AJR, the Association of Jewish Refugees.
I'd never even heard of them.
I'm ashamed to say I didn't know.
So she gave me the address.
I went there.
And I put-- I put it to them, to the-- to the editor.
And he was very-- he's one of the kinder as well.
I'm finding kinder everywhere.
Everywhere I go, you find kinder.
He was very interested, put an article in the journal.
I spoke-- I told the I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sort of publicized, and we never paid a penny for any adverts ever.
I phoned my sister, and she said, oh, that's a very good idea.
And she lives, of course, in Jerusalem.
She has an acquaintance, Paul [?
Cohen ?].
He is a wonderful journalist.
Now, his piece of journalism that was published, a double-page spread in the Jerusalem Post went worldwide.
It is precise, concise.
It is everything anybody ever wants to know about the Kindertransport.
And we reproduced it in our brochure.
Well, that's how it started.
I also wrote a letter to the [?
Harwich ?] Press and-- wanting to know if, in the archives, still a memory exists of us, so many children who arrived here.
And lo and behold, I got so many letters back from them.
They published an article in their press in the [?
Harwich ?] newspaper.
And then I got letters because-- from sailors who remembered us, from an old lady who was a teacher then who came to teach us English, lived across the road in Dover Court, wonderful letters.
And one letter I got with a-- the most famous of all the photographs, of a policeman holding two little children on-- one on either hand.
And she told me that was her father, the policeman who had recently died.
She sent me that photograph, and that started the ball rolling.
I don't know how it happened.
Like topsy, it growed.
It became worldwide.
I found a friend, [?
Paula ?] Hill, who is, you know, who is very clever girl.
She's an archivist.
And she actually became-- she wasn't an archivist to start with.
She became an archivist through this business.
And she researched, and she found dozens of wonderful photographs in what was the former that is [?
Houten ?] Deutsche Company.
They've got all these pictures that were taken when we arrived.
I've got quite a few in here.
But now I've lent them to the Watford Theatre Company only yesterday.
They're going to exhibit it with the play that's coming on in May.
So it started the ball rolling.
And it rolled, and it rolled, and never stopped.
And then we started-- we got the Jewish yearbook.
My husband then was very helpful.
Must say, for a whole year, he was very pleased to help me.
We organized a reunion.
I realized within a couple of weeks that the synagogue wouldn't have been big enough.
I started to research and learn in the I never knew 10,000 children were given permission to enter.
I knew nothing because I'd been locked away for five years.
And afterwards, I was busy.
You raise your family.
You have commitments, and you don't think about that.
We wanted to forget that we were refugees because of the experience in Birmingham.
We-- we all learned English as fast as we could.
Who wants to be a refugee?
It's not very nice to be a refugee.
We all swept it under the carpet.
That was it.
And to come out with it, to have been a refugee, that was a tremendous thing.
A lot of people didn't want to be known having been refugees up to this day.
But now, everybody's proud of being a [? kind ?] on the Kindertransport.
